title: Lemon and poppyseed cake
subtitle: A zesty loaf cake with a sticky lemon drizzle crust
excerpt: A lemon loaf cake made with plenty of zest in the batter, finished with a lemon-juice drizzle poured over while still warm. The drizzle soaks in and sets to a thin, granular sugar crust.
sourceNotes: Lemon drizzle cake is a British baking staple from the mid-20th century, the drizzle method popularised in home baking guides and WI recipe collections from the 1970s and 1980s. Poppyseeds as an addition became common in British domestic baking in the 1990s, drawing on continental European baking where poppyseed cakes are traditional. The recipe here follows the standard British loaf format.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the drizzle, well wrapped. Drizzle after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for 3 days wrapped at room temperature. The crust softens slightly after a day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of lemon juice and granulated sugar poured over a hot cake immediately from the oven while still in its tin. The juice soaks into the warm crumb and the sugar forms a thin, granular crust as it dries. Caster sugar dissolves too fully; granulated sugar retains the texture.

content.text: The drizzle goes on while the cake is hot and still in the tin. This is not optional timing. The warm crumb absorbs the lemon juice; the granulated sugar stays on the surface and dries to the characteristic gritty crust. A cold cake just has wet sugar sitting on top.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Beat the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour and baking powder. Stir in the lemon zest, poppyseeds, and milk. Spoon into the tin and level the surface. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until a skewer comes out clean, the surface is pale gold and set, and the loaf pulls slightly from the sides.
Mix the lemon juice and granulated sugar together in a small bowl. As soon as the cake comes out of the oven, prick the top all over with a skewer. Pour the
slowly over the hot cake, letting it soak in. Leave in the tin until completely cool before turning out. The drizzle crust forms as it dries.
